en.extract:
The green-backed whistler (Pachycephala albiventris) or olive-backed whistler, is a species of bird in the family Pachycephalidae. It is endemic to the Philippines. Its natural habitats are tropical moist lowland forest and tropical moist montane forest.


Description and taxonomy
Formerly, some authorities considered the green-backed whistler to be a subspecies of the mangrove whistler.


Subspecies
Three subspecies are recognized:

P. a. albiventris – (Ogilvie-Grant, 1894): Found on northern Luzon (Philippines)
P. a. crissalis – (Zimmer, JT, 1918): Originally described as a separate species. Found on central and southern Luzon (Philippines)
P. a. mindorensis – (Bourns & Worcester, 1894): Originally described as a separate species. Found on Mindoro (Philippines)


Ecology and behavior
Forages in the understory for insects and often joins mixed-species flocks Blue-headed fantail, Little pied flycatcher, Turquoise flycatcher, Negros leaf warbler and other small birds.


Habitat and conservation status
Its natural habitats are tropical moist lowland forest and tropical moist montane forest up to 2,000 meters above sea level although this species seems to be more common in higher altitudes. The IUCN has classified the species as being of Least Concern where it is said to be locally common. A large portion of its montane habitat to remain intact than lowland forest. However, there it is still affected by habitat loss through deforestation, mining,  land conversion and slash-and-burn.
It is found in multiple protected areas such as Mount Banahaw, Mount Makiling, Mount Isarog and Mounts Iglit–Baco Natural Park but like all areas in the Philippines protection is lax and deforestation continues despite this protection on paper.


References

The '''green-backed whistler''' ('''''Pachycephala albiventris''''') or '''olive-backed whistler''', is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Pachycephalidae]]. It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to the Philippines. Its natural [[habitat]]s are [[subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest|tropical moist lowland forest]] and tropical moist [[montane forest]].

== Description and taxonomy ==
Formerly, some authorities considered the green-backed whistler to be a subspecies of the [[mangrove whistler]].
===Subspecies===
Three [[subspecies]] are recognized:<ref>{{Cite web |title=Whiteheads, sitellas & whistlers « IOC World Bird List |url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/whistlers/ |access-date=2017-01-31 |website=www.worldbirdnames.org |language=en-US}}</ref>
* ''P. a. albiventris'' – <small>([[William Robert Ogilvie-Grant|Ogilvie-Grant]], 1894)</small>: Found on northern [[Luzon]] (Philippines)
* ''P. a. crissalis'' – <small>([[John Todd Zimmer|Zimmer, JT]], 1918)</small>: Originally described as a separate species. Found on central and southern Luzon (Philippines)
* ''P. a. mindorensis'' – <small>([[Frank Swift Bourns|Bourns]] & [[Dean Conant Worcester|Worcester]], 1894)</small>: Originally described as a separate species. Found on [[Mindoro]] (Philippines)

== Ecology and behavior ==
Forages in the understory for insects and often joins mixed-species flocks [[Blue-headed fantail]], [[Little pied flycatcher]], [[Turquoise flycatcher]], [[Negros leaf warbler]] and other small birds.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Boles |first=Walter |date=2020 |title=Green-backed Whistler (Pachycephala albiventris), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/grbwhi1/1.0/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.grbwhi1.01 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

== Habitat and conservation status ==
Its natural [[habitat]]s are [[subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest|tropical moist lowland forest]] and tropical moist [[montane forest]] up to 2,000 meters above sea level although this species seems to be more common in higher altitudes. The [[IUCN]] has classified the species as being of [[Least Concern]] where it is said to be locally common. A large portion of its montane habitat to remain intact than lowland forest. However, there it is still affected by [[habitat loss]] through deforestation, mining,  [[land conversion]] and [[slash-and-burn]].

It is found in multiple protected areas such as [[Mount Banahaw]], [[Mount Makiling]], [[Mount Isarog]] and [[Mounts Iglit–Baco Natural Park]] but like all areas in the Philippines protection is lax and deforestation continues despite this protection on paper.<ref>{{Cite report |url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/22705443/130399514 |title=Pachycephala albiventris: BirdLife International: The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species 2018: e.T22705443A130399514 |last=IUCN |date=2018-08-07 |publisher=International Union for Conservation of Nature |doi=10.2305/iucn.uk.2018-2.rlts.t22705443a130399514.en |language=en}}</ref>

zh.extract:
绿背啸鹟（学名：Pachycephala albiventris）是啸鹟科啸鹟属的一种，为菲律宾的特有种。该物种的保护状况被评为无危。
绿背啸鹟的平均体重约为21.1克。栖息地包括亚热带或热带的湿润低地林和亚热带或热带的湿润山地林。
